chain_name: Oak barrel porosity → micro-oxygenation → tannin polymerisation and colour stabilisation

starting_factor.description: Oak barrel staves are semi-permeable — tiny amounts of oxygen permeate through the wood over time, regardless of the barrel's age or fill level.

intermediate_steps.description: Oxygen ingress through the stave wood is very slow and continuous — approximately 20–40 mg O₂/L/year in a standard 225L barrique. This is dramatically slower than oxygen addition from racking or splashing, allowing gradual rather than sudden oxidation.

Controlled slow oxygen exposure facilitates polymerisation of condensed tannins — short, astringent tannin chains link together into longer polymer chains. Longer tannin polymers fall below the threshold of sensory detection for astringency, making the tannins 'softer' and better integrated.

Oxygen also catalyses acetaldehyde-mediated reactions between anthocyanins (colour pigments) and tannins, producing stable pigment-tannin complexes (pyranoanthocyanins). These complexes resist oxidative fading and SO₂ bleaching, stabilising the wine's colour over time.

final_outcome.description: Softer, more integrated tannin structure; more stable, deeper colour. Old oak barrels provide this micro-oxygenation effect with minimal flavour contribution — making old oak a tool specifically for structural development rather than flavour addition.

examples.region_or_wine: Bordeaux Château barrel ageing (18–24 months in barriques)
examples.description: Progressive polymerisation of Cabernet Sauvignon tannins during barrel ageing transforms the initially extracted, grippy tannins into a silkier, integrated structure ready for bottle ageing.

examples.region_or_wine: Spanish Vino de la Tierra aged in large old oak foudres
examples.description: Large old foudres provide micro-oxygenation without any flavour extraction, allowing producers to soften tannins and stabilise colour without adding an oaky taste to the wine.

related_exam_questions: What is micro-oxygenation and how does it affect wine structure?
Why might a producer use old oak rather than new oak for extended ageing?
How does barrel ageing contribute to tannin integration in red wines?
distinction_note: A Distinction answer specifies the oxygen ingress rate, distinguishes micro-oxygenation from bulk aeration, explains tannin polymerisation (increased chain length → reduced astringency), and describes anthocyanin-tannin complex formation and its effect on colour stability.
